title: The Macro Minute: Are the US and global economies worthy of reinvesting into?

Happy Thursday out there at 13:42, it's
your skipper here at Darius Dale
presenting our macro minute for
Thursday, April 9th, 2026. Hope
everyone's having a great week. So, as
always, we'll start with the executive
summary from today's Lead-Off Morning
Note, so let's dive right in. Today's
key macro question is, are the US and
global economies worthy of reinvesting
into?
Our answer is yes. The latest key
economic data generally supported our
bullish paradigm C, productivity boom,
and source of funds themes. This implies
that investors who reduced exposure to
manage risk early in the US-Israel-Iran
conflict should feel confident that the
GDP and earnings revision cycles are
likely to remain tailwinds over the
medium term. This, of course, is
contingent on the resolution of the
conflict. Refer to our April 8th
Lead-Off Morning Note for details
regarding our sanguine thoughts on that.
In short, investors would do well to
ignore the brinksmanship headlines and
work backward from what is likely to be
positive news flow throughout the Q2
through Q4 earning seasons.
Here are the most important things we
learned today regarding the six key
macro cycles that influence the momentum
and dispersion within and across asset
markets on a trend basis. Starting with
the growth cycle, the strong negative
impulses in real PCE and real goods PCE
in February did not support our
resilient US economy and paradigm C
themes. The weak positive impulse in
real services PCE, however, did support
our resilient US economy and paradigm C
themes. The weak positive impulses in
nominal DPI and real DPI and the decline
in the personal savings rate in February
supported our resilient US economy and
paradigm C themes.
The strong negative impulse in nominal
employee compensation in February
supported our jobless recovery theme.
The weak negative impulse in nominal
wage and salary compensation in February
supported our jobless recovery theme.
The weak negative impulse in real GDP
ex-government and net exports in the
final Q4 GDP data did not support our
paradigm C theme. The strong positive
impulse in net corporate profits in Q4
supported our productivity boom and
jobless recovery themes. The dramatic
unemployment dramatic underperformance
of employment growth relative to net
corporate profits and retained earnings
growth supports our jobless recovery
theme, as well.
Initial jobless claims currently
signaling a low probability of a
developing recession in the US economy,
as do the 42 macro Fab 5 recession
signaling indicators and the 42 macro
business cycle model. In the Eurozone,
the February real retail sales data
supported our outlook for moderating
growth. In Japan, the March economy
watches and consumer confidence survey
data did not support our outlook for
firming growth. The March machine tool
orders data, however, did support our
outlook for firming growth. Recall that
Japan machine tool orders are among the
best leading indicators of the global
manufacturing cycle and continue to
signal a cyclical uptrend. Shifting to
the inflation cycle, the strong positive
impulses in the headline, core, and
supercore PCE deflators in February
supported our sticky inflation theme.
The three-month annualized rate of
change for each metric is
[clears throat] at least 4% signaling an
inflation problem that warranted a
hawkish repricing of the Fed's reaction
function before the US-Israel-Iran
conflict began.
The weak positive impulse in the housing
PCE deflator in February did not support
our cooling housing and labor theme. In
the Eurozone, the February PPI data
supported our outlook for moderating
inflation. Nothing actionable with
respect to our Bayesian priors for the
monetary policy cycle, fiscal policy
cycle, liquidity cycle, and positioning
cycle. So, let's transition to our 42
macro dashboard. As always, we'll wrap
up with the question from our global
investor community. This one's titled
[clears throat] Freedom of the Seas.
If the US engages in a toll for passage
through the Strait of Hormuz,
what stops other countries from placing
tolls on ocean choke points anywhere
they can? Doesn't this undermine free
trade? Uh so, the the
the quick answer is nothing is going to
stop them outside of the US, you know,
naval intervention or NATO naval
intervention, but the reality
[clears throat] is is, you know, the
countries that would benefit most from
free trade generally don't want this
outcome. So, uh it's unlikely that
they're incentivized to to do this. Uh
but what the going back to this
undermining of free trade thought
process, this is something we've been
coaching our global investor community
and and to prepare for uh really since
we first authored our Investing in a
Fourth Turning Regime Framework back in
the summer of 2023. Uh if we can take it
to our uh macro scouting report here
going to that Investing in a Fourth
Turning Regime uh section of those
monthly presentations, you know, one of
the things we uh highlighted in that
presentation that inaugural presentation
I've consistently highlighted uh since
is that trade tends to decline sharply
during fourth turnings. We're in the
middle of fourth turning and this chart
needs to be updated, but I would imagine
we're probably down here now in terms of
trade trade as a percentage of global
GDP and this is something that's likely
to continue uh to be ongoing for at
least another, you know, 5 to 7, 5 to 10
years. And as trade declines, another
dynamic that we've been uh coaching and
and helping investors prepare for is the
possibility and probability that uh war
uh and just geopolitical hot
geopolitical conflict accelerates. Um
you know, historically, you know, you
study these cycles and study
civilizations, when trade goes down, war
goes up, conflict goes up. So, investors
should continue to prepare for uh
geopolitical conflict uh and all the
different uh ramifications for that
across um global bond markets, uh global
stock and equity stock and credit
markets, uh foreign exchange reserve
flows, uh etc. So, uh if you want some
more thoughts on that, um come check us
out. I'll have wrap it up there. Darius
Dale here uh presenting our uh macro
minute for uh Thursday, April 9th, 2026.
Best of luck out there today. Uh we'll
catch back here on Monday. I'm going to
be offline traveling uh tomorrow, so
have a good weekend. Cheers.
